# **Contrastive Neuron Attribution and Activation Steering in Large Language Models**

Mechanistic interpretability seeks to decode the high-dimensional internal representations of transformer models into discrete, human-understandable computational circuits. Traditional activation steering approaches operate by shifting activations along a low-dimensional direction in the residual stream. While effective, these residual-stream interventions act as coarse, global modifications that frequently push the model’s internal states off the natural data manifold, resulting in severe degradation of output quality and linguistic coherence at high steering strengths.1 Recent advances have introduced Contrastive Neuron Attribution (CNA), a localized post-hook steering framework that identifies and modulates highly sparse circuits of individual Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) neurons.1 This review provides a technical analysis of the mathematical foundations of CNA, details the Layer-wise Relevance Propagation (LRP) rules required to linearize the transformer backward pass, compares its efficacy against alternative steering paradigms, and explores structural changes across base and instruction-tuned architectures.

## **Foundations of Contrastive Discovery and Underlying Mathematics**

Contrastive Neuron Attribution is built on the empirical observation that abstract semantic concepts and behavioral directives are represented sparsely within the individual neuron bases of a model's MLP layers rather than being broadly diffused throughout the entire residual stream.1 By isolating the specific hidden units that display maximum activation variance when exposed to contrasting stimuli, CNA identifies highly localized circuits responsible for distinct behavioral features, such as safety refusals, stylistic alignment, or factual recall.3  
To isolate a target behavioral circuit, a contrastive discovery set is constructed.2 This dataset consists of a set of positive prompts ![][image1] that exhibit the target behavioral trait (e.g., harmful queries or sycophantic statements) and a matched set of negative prompts ![][image2] that represent the benign counterpart or opposite behavior.1 These inputs are fed through the language model in a series of forward passes.1 For each prompt, MLP activations are recorded at the final token position using forward pre-hooks registered on the down-projection layer (down\_proj) of each MLP block.1 The final token position is selected because it acts as the computational bottleneck that encapsulates the consolidated context before output generation.1  
Let ![][image3] denote the post-activation value of neuron ![][image4] in layer ![][image5] when the model processes prompt ![][image6].1 The mean contrastive difference ![][image7] for each individual neuron across the positive and negative sets is formulated as:  
![][image8]  
Once ![][image7] is computed for all neurons across all MLP layers, the target behavioral circuit ![][image9] is isolated by selecting the top ![][image10] neurons ranked by the absolute magnitude of their contrastive differences 1:  
![][image11]  
Empirical evaluations across varying model scales establish that setting the threshold ![][image10] to ![][image12] of the total MLP activations (typically yielding ![][image13] to ![][image14] active neurons) is sufficient to control target behaviors.1 Causality is verified at inference time by intercepting the forward pass and multiplying the activation of each neuron ![][image15] by a scalar multiplier ![][image16].1 This scaling paradigm operates as:  
![][image17]  
Clamping the selected neurons to zero (![][image18]) ablates the target behavior, whereas setting ![][image19] amplifies it.1 This contrastive discovery approach requires only forward passes, completely avoiding the need for GPU gradient calculations or auxiliary model training.1  
Alternative methodologies have sought to model these dynamics by capturing functional antagonism within network structures. For instance, the NeuronLLM framework introduces Question-Answering-based Task Transformation (QATT) and Contrastive Neuron Identification (CNI) to evaluate the opposing roles of supportive and inhibitive neurons.5 CNI leverages a cross-entropy-based contrastive scoring method to evaluate how individual neurons influence the confidence of correct options versus the uncertainty of incorrect options under a closed set of alternative completions.6 While frameworks like NeuronLLM operate via cross-entropy optimization to balance functional antagonism, CNA relies on direct activation variance, trading optimization complexity for structural simplicity.1

## **Mathematical Formulations of Transformer-Specific LRP Rules**

While behavioral features can be isolated via forward contrastive passes, single-prompt factual attribution (such as tracing the exact path of knowledge retrieval) relies on Layer-wise Relevance Propagation (LRP).4 LRP is a backward propagation approach subject to conservation properties, redistributing activation values backward to the input space based on predefined local rules.7 Standard LRP rules developed for feed-forward neural networks fail when applied to modern autoregressive transformers due to non-linear operations like Layer Normalization, Scaled Dot-Product Attention (SDPA), and gated MLPs.9 To maintain relevance conservation and prevent gradient shattering, the neural-steering framework employs three dedicated LRP rules to linearize the backward pass.4

### **The LN-rule for RMSNorm Linearization**

Root Mean Square Normalization (RMSNorm) stabilizes training by scaling activations based on their root mean square value.4 For an input vector ![][image20], the forward pass is represented as:  
![][image21]  
![][image22]  
where ![][image23] is a learnable scaling parameter and ![][image24] is a small float to prevent division by zero. Standard backpropagation through the divisor ![][image25] introduces significant cross-dimension noise, as the derivative of the normalization term distributes gradient signals across unrelated token representations, leading to gradient diffusion. To prevent this noise while preserving token-specific scale properties, the LN-rule detaches the normalization coefficient during the backward pass 4:  
![][image26]  
![][image27]  
By treating ![][image28] as a constant during the backward pass, the derivative simplifies directly to:  
![][image29]  
This formulation decouples cross-token scaling dependencies, ensuring that relevance scores propagate backward through the normalization layers without experiencing numerical diffusion or attenuation.4

### **The AH-rule for Attention Backpropagation**

Standard modern transformers utilize Scaled Dot-Product Attention (SDPA) or FlashAttention, which fuse softmax calculations, masking, and matrix multiplications into optimized GPU kernels. These fused operations act as black boxes for automatic differentiation, rendering intermediate relevance tracking through attention heads impossible.  
The AH-rule resolves this by bypassing SDPA and FlashAttention during the attribution pass, forcing the model to execute eager, step-by-step attention calculations.4 The eager formulation computes the attention weights and outputs explicitly:  
![][image30]  
![][image31]  
By materializing the attention matrix ![][image32] in memory, the backward pass can access the exact intermediate Jacobians of the softmax function. This allows the attribution algorithm to backpropagate relevance scores cleanly through the Query (![][image33]), Key (![][image34]), Value (![][image35]), and Output (![][image36]) projection matrices, avoiding the gradient shattering associated with fused kernels.4

### **The Half-rule for Gated MLP Attributions**

Modern architectures employ gated MLP blocks (such as SwiGLU) instead of traditional feed-forward networks. The forward pass of a gated MLP activation layer is defined as:  
![][image37]  
![][image38]  
In this layer, the final output is derived from an elementwise multiplication between the gating projection and the up-projection activations.4 Applying standard gradient propagation to a product ![][image39] yields ![][image40] and ![][image41]. This gradient profile is highly sensitive to local scaling; if the gate activation is near zero, the up-projection receives no gradient, and vice versa.  
To stabilize attribution through this multiplicative bottleneck, the Half-rule applies a symmetric Shapley 50/50 division to the elementwise multiplication.4 Under this rule, rather than calculating asymmetric local derivatives, the incoming relevance ![][image42] is split equally between the two factor branches 4:  
![][image43]  
This linearizes the non-linear elementwise multiplication, ensuring that both the gate projection and the up-projection receive an equal share of the backward attribution regardless of their relative forward-pass magnitudes.4

## **Behavioral Circuits and Sparse Interventions**

Contrastive Neuron Attribution isolates highly sparse circuits of about ![][image12] of total MLP activations (![][image13]–![][image14] neurons) to control target behaviors.1 This sparse intervention can target safety refusals, sycophancy, or factual recall.1 For example, factual steering utilizes single-prompt circuit discovery (RelP attribution) to isolate a factual circuit (such as a "capitals" circuit 4) by backpropagating relevance from a specific target token (e.g., "Austin" for Texas 4).  
Unlike residual-stream methods like Contrastive Activation Addition (CAA), which modify the entire layer-wide signal 1, CNA operates directly within the model's native neuron basis, modifying only the scale of the specific units dedicated to that concept.1 This localized intervention preserves the model's overall representational geometry.1 Additionally, while Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) also decompose activations into interpretable features, they require training massive auxiliary networks on billions of tokens, making them computationally expensive.2 CNA achieves high precision using simple contrastive forward passes or a single linearized backward pass.1  
To demonstrate this effect, the table below compiles empirical results from the neural-steering framework evaluating refusal reduction and generation quality (measured as the complement of the fraction of repeated n-grams) under maximum intervention strength (![][image44]).1

| Model Name | Baseline Refusal % | CNA Refusal % | CNA Quality Score | CAA Refusal % | CAA Quality Score |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct** 4 | 90.0% | 20.2% | 0.975 | 0.0% | 0.554 |
| **Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct** 4 | 84.0% | 26.3% | 0.977 | 0.0% | 0.431 |
| **Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct** 4 | 90.0% | 5.1% | 0.969 | 38.4% | 0.493 |
| **Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct** 4 | 86.0% | 12.1% | 0.981 | 0.0% | 0.569 |
| **Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct** 4 | 93.0% | 26.3% | 0.982 | 100.0% | 0.888 |
| **Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct** 4 | 90.0% | 34.3% | 0.984 | 0.0% | 0.844 |
| **Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct** 4 | 87.0% | 13.1% | 0.980 | 5.1% | 0.414 |
| **Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct** 4 | 78.0% | 5.1% | 0.983 | 98.0% | 0.406 |

The empirical data demonstrates that while CAA can drive the refusal rate to absolute zero in several architectures, it causes a severe drop in output quality—frequently falling below ![][image45].2 CNA, by contrast, reduces refusal rates by over ![][image46] (and up to ![][image47] in larger models like Qwen2.5-72B) while consistently maintaining output quality scores above ![][image48].1

## **Base and Instruct Model Circuit Dynamics**

A critical discovery in the mechanistic analysis of language models is the structural difference between base models and their instruction-tuned counterparts.1 Applying the CNA discovery pipeline to matched base and instruct variants reveals a unique architectural dichotomy: the spatial layer-wise structure of behavioral discrimination exists in both models, but the causal functionality of individual neurons is entirely transformed by alignment fine-tuning.1  
In both base and instruct variants of Llama and Qwen models, CNA consistently isolates the top contrastive neurons in the late layers of the network.1 This spatial concentration demonstrates that the capacity to representationally distinguish between harmful and benign concepts is not a byproduct of safety alignment; rather, the base model naturally develops late-layer features to classify the semantic nature of the input text during pre-training.2  
However, the causal role of these representations differs between the two variants. Steering the late-layer contrastive neurons in a base model produces only content shifts.1 For example, amplifying these neurons might bias the model to generate text containing safety-related keywords, synonyms, or general thematic rephrasings, but it does not alter the underlying behavior or elicit structured refusals.1 The representation remains a passive semantic classifier.2 After instruction-tuning, these exact late-layer structures become highly sensitive, causal refusal gates.1 Modulating the discovered circuit directly controls the model's behavioral response, flipping it between compliance and refusal.1  
This functional divergence is explained by the micro-level rewiring of individual neurons.2 While the broad, macro-level layer structures are preserved, fine-tuning fundamentally replaces the specific neurons that compose the circuit.2 Analysis of the overlap of specific (layer, neuron) index pairs between matched base and instruct circuits reveals that only ![][image49] to ![][image50] of individual neurons are shared.2  
This suggests that safety alignment does not construct a novel representation manifold.2 Instead, it hooks into pre-existing passive classification manifolds in the late layers, replacing their constituent active units to connect those semantic representations directly to downstream behavioral execution engines.2  
The table below presents base vs. instruct comparison metrics, demonstrating how steering base models fails to act as a safety gate and instead causes severe quality degradation.4

| Model Variant | Baseline Refusal % | Steered Refusal % (m=0.0) | Generation Quality Score |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Llama-3.2-1B Base** 4 | 2.0% | 0.0% | 0.658 |
| **Llama-3.2-1B Instruct** 4 | 43.4% | 20.2% | 0.975 |
| **Qwen2.5-3B Base** 4 | 14.1% | 11.1% | 0.865 |
| **Qwen2.5-3B Instruct** 4 | 92.9% | 34.3% | 0.984 |

As displayed, steering the pre-existing late-layer structure in base models fails to produce functional steering and causes severe quality degradation (quality drops to 0.658 and 0.865), whereas steering the instruct model's circuit reduces refusal rates while keeping quality exceptionally high (0.975 and 0.984).4

## **Known Limitations and Infrastructure Filtering**

Despite its precision, Contrastive Neuron Attribution is subject to specific failure modes and implementation constraints that must be accounted for to ensure robust behavioral control.

### **Steering Drift and Capability Interference**

When applying high multiplier strengths (![][image51]) to amplify a specific behavioral circuit, models can experience steering drift or collateral capability degradation.4 This occurs because individual neurons are rarely perfectly monofunctional.11 A neuron that primarily acts as a gate for safety refusals may also participate in secondary polyfunctional circuits responsible for specialized coding syntax, translation, or grammatical conjugation.11 Amplifying these shared nodes can suppress or distort unrelated model capabilities, introducing spelling errors, syntactic rigidity, or thematic stagnation in the model's output.4

### **The Universal Infrastructure Neuron Problem**

A primary obstacle in contrastive neuron discovery is the presence of universal, task-agnostic "infrastructure" neurons.4 These are hidden units that fire consistently at extremely high levels across almost all forward passes, regardless of the prompt's semantic content.1 These neurons typically govern low-level operational tasks, such as tracking token positions, handling syntax boundaries, managing carriage returns, or processing common punctuation.4  
Because infrastructure neurons exhibit massive raw activation levels, slight variations in their baseline firing rates can produce large absolute mean differences (![][image7]) during contrastive discovery.2 If left unfiltered, these universal nodes will dominate the top ![][image12] circuit selection.2 Ablating or amplifying them does not affect the target behavior, but instead destabilizes the model's fundamental generation infrastructure, leading to syntax errors, endless loops, or complete gibberish.1

### **Best Practices for Automated Blacklisting**

To isolate pure behavioral circuits, task-agnostic infrastructure neurons must be identified and removed from the candidate pool before circuit construction.2 The neural-steering library automates this using a universal filtering protocol 4:

1. **Diverse Calibration Run:** A broad calibration dataset of diverse, completely unrelated prompts is processed through the model (e.g., standard coding queries, historical descriptions, math problems, and conversational small talk).2  
2. **Top-k Activation Tracking:** For each calibration prompt, the top ![][image12] of active MLP neurons are recorded.1  
3. **Frequency Thresholding:** Any neuron that appears in the top ![][image12] of active units for ![][image52] or more of the diverse calibration prompts is classified as a task-agnostic infrastructure neuron.1  
4. **Blacklist Exclusion:** These flagged units are added to a global blacklist and excluded from all contrastive circuits.2

By filtering out these universal background units, the remaining circuit represents only the precise causal mechanism responsible for the target behavior, ensuring safe and stable behavioral steering even at high intervention strengths.2

#### **Works cited**

1. Targeted Neuron Modulation via Contrastive Pair Search \- arXiv, accessed on May 24, 2026, [https://arxiv.org/html/2605.12290v1](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.12290v1)  
2. Nous Research Releases Contrastive Neuron Attribution (CNA): Sparse MLP Circuit Steering Without SAE Training or Weight Modification \- MarkTechPost, accessed on May 24, 2026, [https://www.marktechpost.com/2026/05/23/nous-research-releases-contrastive-neuron-attribution-cna-sparse-mlp-circuit-steering-without-sae-training-or-weight-modification/](https://www.marktechpost.com/2026/05/23/nous-research-releases-contrastive-neuron-attribution-cna-sparse-mlp-circuit-steering-without-sae-training-or-weight-modification/)

4. NousResearch/neural-steering: Implementation of Contrastive Neuron Attribution for behavioral detection and steering. \- GitHub, accessed on May 24, 2026, [https://github.com/NousResearch/neural-steering](https://github.com/NousResearch/neural-steering)  
5. Identifying Good and Bad Neurons for Task-Level Controllable LLMs \- arXiv, accessed on May 24, 2026, [https://arxiv.org/html/2601.04548v2](https://arxiv.org/html/2601.04548v2)  
6. Identifying Good and Bad Neurons for Task-Level Controllable LLMs | OpenReview, accessed on May 24, 2026, [https://openreview.net/forum?id=PXZ3V2CxHr](https://openreview.net/forum?id=PXZ3V2CxHr)

8. Revisiting LRP: Positional Attribution as the Missing Ingredient for Transformer Explainability, accessed on May 24, 2026, [https://neurips.cc/virtual/2025/poster/117168](https://neurips.cc/virtual/2025/poster/117168)  
9. AttnLRP: Attention-Aware Layer-Wise Relevance Propagation for Transformers \- GitHub, accessed on May 24, 2026, [https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mlresearch/v235/main/assets/achtibat24a/achtibat24a.pdf](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mlresearch/v235/main/assets/achtibat24a/achtibat24a.pdf)
